name: Roc Morphic — Borrow Inference and Alias Analysis
summary: Borrow-based RC inference plus alias analysis used by Roc to eliminate most inc/dec ops before lowering. The upstream `morphic-lang/morphic` is the cleaner reference implementation; in Roc, a December 2024 alias-analysis bug around `joinpoint`/`jump` loops prompted PR #7370 to limit Morphic to trivial analysis until fixed.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Roc combines **Morphic** (borrow-based RC inference, eliminates most inc/dec) + Perceus reuse + LLVM. Morphic is an alias-and-borrow analysis: it propagates per-binding flags about whether a reference is borrowed (no RC traffic needed) or owned (drop emits a `dec`), and whether two references may alias (constraining where in-place mutation is safe).

This is a different angle on the same compile-time RC problem solved by Perceus, Lean 4, and Lobster: rather than inserting `inc`/`dec` from a uniqueness proof, Morphic inserts them from a *borrow* proof. The two are complementary, and Roc runs Perceus reuse on top of Morphic's borrow output.

Morphic is documented as `data/roc-morphic` (this record); Roc's *lambda-set / defunctionalisation* angle is documented in `data/roc-lambda-sets`. The two analyses interact (lambda-set inference shapes what Morphic can see) but are conceptually separate.

sections.title: Borrow and alias inference
sections.content:
The reference implementation lives in the upstream `morphic-lang/morphic` repository — cleaner than the Roc embedding because it is studied in isolation rather than entangled with Roc's lambda-set machinery and platform glue. Two passes:

- **Borrow inference** — annotates each function parameter and let-binding with whether it is borrowed (caller retains ownership; callee may not consume) or owned (callee gets the only handle and is responsible for the drop). Borrowed references emit no RC traffic; owned references get an explicit `dec` at end-of-life.
- **Alias analysis** — propagates may-alias information so the reuse analyser (Perceus-style) knows when a destructive write to a refcount-1 cell is safe. Two references that may alias cannot both be treated as unique writers.

The combination eliminates most `inc`/`dec` operations before they reach LLVM, and the residual RC ops feed into Perceus's reuse pass for in-place updates.

sections.title: December 2024 alias-bug and PR #7370 limit
sections.content:
A Morphic alias-analysis bug surfaced in December 2024 (issue #7367): incorrect in-place mutation when `joinpoint`/`jump` form loops in Roc's IR. The analysis treated the back-edge as if it preserved uniqueness, allowing a destructive write that aliased a still-live reference reaching the join point on the next iteration.

PR #7370 *limited Morphic to trivial analysis* until the bug was fixed — a useful lesson on the difficulty of proving uniqueness across CFG back-edges. Until the more aggressive analysis is restored, Roc trades some inc/dec elision for soundness.

Status (as of 2026-04): the trivial-analysis fallback is the operative behaviour in Roc. The upstream `morphic-lang/morphic` repository remains the reference for the full design. The episode reinforces a broader observation in the compile-time RC literature (Perceus reuse, Lean 4 `isExclusive`, Lobster lifetime analysis): proving uniqueness inside ordinary acyclic CFGs is tractable; doing so across loop back-edges and join points requires a much more careful argument, and getting the fixed-point wrong silently corrupts the heap.
